title: Forgiveness
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Forgiveness
summary: Forgiveness in Scripture is both a gift received from God and a practice extended to others — costly, often difficult, but essential to spiritual freedom and relational healing.
body:
When Peter asked Jesus how many times he should forgive — suggesting the generous number of seven — Jesus answered seventy times seven [verse:matt.18.21-22]. The math is not literal. The point is that forgiveness is not a quota but a posture.

Jesus followed with a parable about a servant forgiven an astronomical debt who then refused to forgive a colleague's small one [verse:matt.18.23-35]. The logic is devastating: those who have been forgiven much cannot withhold forgiveness from others without profound hypocrisy.

Paul grounds forgiveness in theology: forgive one another as God in Christ forgave you [verse:eph.4.32]. The standard is not fairness but grace — forgiving because you have been forgiven, not because the offender deserves it.

Joseph's story provides the most sustained Old Testament example. Betrayed by his brothers, sold into slavery, and imprisoned for years, Joseph eventually stood before those same brothers and wept. His words reframe the entire ordeal: you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good [verse:gen.50.20]. Forgiveness did not erase the harm. It refused to let the harm have the final word.

Forgiveness does not require pretending the offense did not happen. It does not necessarily restore trust. What it does is release the forgiver from the prison of resentment [verse:col.3.13].

verses.annotation: Seventy times seven — forgiveness as an unlimited posture, not a counted transaction.
The parable of the unforgiving servant — those forgiven much must forgive much.
Forgive as God in Christ forgave you — grace as both source and standard.
Joseph reframes his brothers' betrayal — evil intended, good accomplished.
Bear with one another and forgive — as the Lord forgave you, so you also must forgive.
Jesus forgives his executioners from the cross — forgiveness at its most costly.
As far as east from west — God's forgiveness removes sin completely.

scholars.quote: Keener explains that Jesus' 'seventy times seven' directly inverts Lamech's boast of seventy-sevenfold vengeance in Genesis 4:24 — Jesus replaces the escalation of revenge with the escalation of grace.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)

scholars.quote: Sarna reads Joseph's 'God meant it for good' not as a dismissal of his brothers' guilt but as a theological reframing — Joseph affirms both human responsibility and divine sovereignty without collapsing one into the other.
Calvin insists that forgiveness does not require the restoration of trust or the absence of consequences — it means releasing the personal claim to vengeance and entrusting justice to God.
